summary: Jeff Atwood examines Christopher Seiwald's 'Seven Pillars of Pretty Code' principles, which argue that visually appealing code tends to work better. While acknowledging the advice is reasonable, Atwood critiques Seiwald's own example code (make.c) as ugly, noting issues like embedded source control comments, fragile block alignments, and poor function naming. The post concludes by questioning whether focusing on cosmetic code formatting misses the bigger picture—that using higher-level languages might be a more meaningful pursuit of code beauty.
key_points: Code formatting preferences are subjective and language-dependent—what looks pretty in one language may look ugly to developers of another
Seiwald's seven principles include keeping columns narrow, breaking code into logical blocks, reducing distractions, and maintaining left-edge structure
Embedded source control information in comments is redundant when source control systems exist
Complex block alignments in code are fragile and difficult to maintain without IDE support
External cosmetic formatting may matter less than internal qualities like choosing appropriate abstraction levels
money_quotes: Code that looks good, works good.
Nothing obscures the essential visual cues more than a shift in style.
Even if your code is inscrutable and your comments jibberish, the reader can at least attempt to triangulate on the actual purpose.
The tighter the scope, the shorter the name. Long, descriptive names may help the first time reader but hinder him every time thereafter.
Clearly, pretty code is in the eye of the beholder.
I'm sure Ruby, Lisp, and Smalltalk aficionados feel the same way about C# code. I know most C# developers can barely force themselves to even look at VB.NET code.
Rather than focusing on the external, cosmetic attributes of the code, shouldn't we be searching for something prettier on the inside? Such as a higher level language?
key_insight: Pretty code formatting is subjective and language-dependent, and obsessing over cosmetic appearance may distract from more fundamental improvements like choosing better abstractions or higher-level languages.
